entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Aerosoft/Toliss A346 Pro “to be Updated soon”, says Aerosoft
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. On the first day of FSWeekend, Aerosoft talked about their newly released A340-600 Pro, built in partnership with Toliss, and their plans for the future regarding updates and improvements. As promised during one of their official streams, the preighter (P2F) model is going to be “one of the next updates”, completely free of charge for current and future owners. The wing model - a point of criticism that was brought up multiple times during the first preview streams - will be rebuilt to look closer to the real counterpart, and the fuselage model will also undergo improvements. Furthermore, the Electronic Flight Bag will see improvements, with the charts feature set to undergo major tweaks, including the ability to pin charts. An issue with incorrect vertical speed and landing rate readings at touchdown was also acknowledged, and tweaks will be implemented in the future to ensure correct figures. They have also made sure to reinforce that they have been listening - and gathering - user feedback to address it when possible. ​ The first update is already undergoing testing and should be released “maybe within the next week,” and the current discount will end once that update is released. ​ Some improvements from the MSFS version will be carried over to X-Plane in future updates. ​ It’s available on Aerosoft’s website for roughly $80.75, requiring at least 18 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Meridian GMT Announces New Product Lineup
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. Meridian GMT has taken the opportunity during FSWeekend 2026’s first day to announce a new product lineup, including a hybrid throttle quadrant and flight control unit, a new yoke, and two-purpose-built flight sim controllers. The Latitude Plus ($350) is their new multi-purpose throttle quadrant, offering four variants for distinct purposes: to begin with, there’s an Airbus-like (Toulouse) design and a Boeing-like (Everett) design, each with multi- and single-engine options. Furthermore, they can be fitted with a servo motor to motorize the trim wheel and throttles, and spoilers for an additional $49.99. The Horizon Pro ($599.99) is their new dual-axis force feedback yoke system, set to launch in early 2027 with a built-in FCU and other useful controls, including lights, basic MFD interaction buttons, and more. ​ The X-Ray Plus ($149.99) is their new purpose-built Xbox controller, engineered for flight simulation, bringing two distinct configurations: airplanes and helicopters, and their equivalent set of required buttons. Landing gear control, flaps, trim, all from one single controller, sized just the same as your average Xbox controller. The design is still subject to change. Please refer to Meridian’s website for further information. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Navigraph Showcases Three New Features
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. Navigraph used its FSWeekend 2026 stage slot to preview three new additions coming soon to Navigraph Charts: Approach Feathers, Extended RWY Centerlines, and a new Measurement Tool. Navigraph has also been running live demos at the show throughout the weekend. Of the three, Approach Feathers is likely the headline feature. It is designed to surface key approach information directly on the chart, including items such as approach type, course, frequency, and identifier, helping reduce workload during the arrival phase. The ability to project the Extended RWY Centerline, meanwhile, is also a handy feature arriving in Navigraph Charts in the future. The third addition, the Measurement Tool, will let users measure distance either directly from point to point, or across multiple selected points, which should make it useful for everything from quick situational checks to more manual route planning. All three features are expected in the next Navigraph Charts release, which the presentation described as coming soon. ‍ Follow Threshold on Sunday, March 22, for more exciting news from FlightSimWeekend 2026. ‍ ‍If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Day 1 and Day 2 Recap
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. FlightSimWeekend 2026 is now well underway in Lelystad, and, after Friday’s Captain’s Dinner and Flight Sim Awards, Saturday brought the first full public day of the show, complete with long queues, a packed theatre, and plenty to see on the floor. Friday, March 20, was centred around the Captain’s Dinner, followed by the Flight Sim Awards 2026. Hosted by KatiePilot and NickFlightX, with a pre-show by TwoToneMurphy, the evening mixed some very good food, music, community, and, of course, the awards. Among the notable winners were FlyByWire’s A380X, the Zibo 737 Mod, iniBuilds’ A350 Airliner, FlightFactor’s 777 Freighter, and Fenix Simulations. Saturday morning then kicked off the show proper at the Aviodrome, and it began in very, let’s say, Dutch fashion, with thick fog and low visibility outside the venue. Even so, the lines were long before opening. On stage, Microsoft opened the festivities. Although Jorg Neumann could not attend in person at the last minute, Microsoft still delivered a keynote confirming City Update 14: The Netherlands and Belgium, which is now available free of charge for MSFS 2020 and MSFS 2024. The team also confirmed that Aircraft and Avionics Update 4 will arrive with Sim Update 5 in a few weeks, bringing updates to the ATR, Antonov An-2, Fokker F.VII, Latecoere 631, and Savoia-Marchetti S.55. On top of that, Microsoft introduced new Avidyne avionics options, and surprise-released the Fokker F27 Friendship by iniBuilds as Local Legend 23. WinCTRL brought a large hardware showcase, including a new A320 overhead panel, new joystick bases, yoke, updated rudder pedals, and more. BlueBird Simulations shared a new trailer for its long-awaited Boeing 757-200, while reaffirming a Q3 2026 release window. Navigraph previewed three upcoming Charts features, Approach Feathers, Extended RWY Centerlines, and a Measurement Tool. Meridian GMT introduced some new hardware of its own, Aerosoft talked about Stuttgart for MSFS 2024 and future A340-600 Pro updates, and MOZA rounded things off with several new cockpit products. Away from the theatre, we spent the day moving around the venue, speaking to exhibitors, filming products, and taking in the views. We checked out everything from Microsoft and MOZA to OSM Aviation Academy, and one of the more eye-catching setups on the floor was perhaps HomeSim’s ProSim A320 family cockpit. We also recorded interviews with Vector, ahead of their Sunday session on the Project Griffin 787, and with WinCTRL, which has arrived at this year’s show with a very large product line-up. Elsewhere, Honeycomb even hosted a Kahoot session for attendees, with prizes up for grabs, adding a bit of extra fun between the larger stage presentations. FSWeekend 2026 has had a strong start. With Sunday still to come, there is still lots more on the way. Stay tuned on Threshold for all the latest from the event. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Aerosoft Releases Stuttgart Airport for MSFS 2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Aerosoft has recently released their rendition of Stuttgart Airport (EDDS) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, serving the city of Stuttgart, Germany, with an average of 7 million passengers per year. Built in 1939 to replace Böblingen Airport, it was taken over by the American forces right after the war. It was partially returned in 1948: the runway and facilities were shared with the US Army during the Cold War. The original terminal was replaced in 2004 with four terminals capable of handling up to 12 million passengers annually. Eurowings and Turkish Airlines use Terminal 1; Terminal 2 is used by Lufthansa and Star Alliance partners; Terminal 3 is used by TUIfly and KLM, and Terminal 4 is a separate building for other airlines, set to be rebuilt and expanded in the future. ​It’s a hub for Condor, Eurowings, and TUI fly Deutschland, sporting a substantial list of destinations. The scenery features an accurate rendition of the airport, with custom ground textures, custom jetways, a high-fidelity terminal interior, and much more. Furthermore, there’s an option to also acquire a GA bundle along with the airport, bringing 6 airfields from the vicinity (EDTQ, ED41, EDZT, EDSV, EDSG, and EDST). The airport is available on Aerosoft’s shop for $24.99, and the bundle with the GA sceneries retails for $29.99. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Vector Shares New 787 Details, Confirms -8 and -10
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Vector returned on March 23 to the stage at FSWeekend 2026, giving a further update on its upcoming Boeing 787, codenamed “Project Griffin”. The update gave a clearer picture of both technical direction and the envisioned roadmap. The newly announced studio, revealed only weeks ago, describes itself as a team of more than 20 developers, including real-world 787 pilots, engineers, and experienced developers. According to Vector, development has been ongoing for over a year. During the presentation, Ellie, Head of Vector Studios, showcased the external modelling of the 787-9, including close-up engine detail, as well as some additional visuals. The aircraft is also being designed with Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024’s walkaround mode in mind, allowing users to interact with panels and inspect the aircraft externally. The presentation had a clear emphasis on system depth. Vector described its approach to the project as “schematic-level simulation”, modelling all aircraft systems based on schematics and real-world documentation, which was illustrated by several technical charts shown on stage. The 787's EDW-technology (Electronically Dimmable Windows) is also being recreated, shown below. Vector extensively referenced access to training and maintenance manuals, alongside input from pilots and ground crew, to support this level of detail. They showed off the electrical and fuel system layouts, as well as pages from the synoptic displays in-sim. Vector also confirmed integration with SimBrief and Navigraph, with users able to choose between the in-sim EFB or external services for flight planning. In a short Q&A session, Vector outlined plans for a configurable failures system, including both manual and random failures, supported by extensive checklists and maintenance pages. Persistent aircraft state is also being explored, although no firm details were provided. Additionally, early work is underway on SATCOM functionality, though the team noted that this remains an area of ongoing development. Looking ahead, Vector plans to share regular “snapshot” updates, focusing on individual areas such as systems, visuals, and soundscape. When confronted about the release timeline, Vector reaffirmed a 2026 release window. While no specific timeframe was given, Vector stated confidence in that target, noting that significant planning had taken place prior to development. Vector also confirmed that the 787-8 and 787-10, are planned. ‍ Keep yourself updated by joining the Vector Studio Discord. See the full presentation here. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FSWeekend 2026: Day 3 Recap
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. After a very busy event, the final day of FSWeekend 2026 was slightly more relaxed, with fewer major announcements, but still plenty to see across the showfloor. The main highlight from the theatre was perhaps Vector’s presentation, where the team shared further details on its Boeing 787, codenamed Project Griffin. The presentation focused on systems modelling, development strategy, and some long-term plans, including confirmation that the 787-8 and 787-10 are also planned for development. Read more on the Vector 787 here. The theatre, of course, continued with presentations from Virtual-Fly, Tailstrike.io, ExcellAir, and FlyByWire Simulations. Away from the stage, the final day was largely about taking in the show itself. The team spent time across the exhibition floor, speaking with developers and hardware manufacturers, including Navigraph, Just Flight, Skalarki, Meridian, StratoWare and others. We will feature some of these soon. We also recorded an interview with MOZA, which will be published on the site in the future. Outside the main halls, visitors had the opportunity to view historic aircraft on display, such as PH-BUK, a KLM Boeing 747-200M, alongside other aircraft, including the Fokker 100, MiG-21 and a Saab 37 Viggen. With that, Flight Sim Weekend 2026 comes to a close. This year’s event once again delivered a fantastic show in Lelystad, and we hope you enjoyed our coverage. We will have additional video coverage from the event coming soon. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Orbx Shares Atlanta International Airport Development Update for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Orbx has recently shared more details about their ongoing work on Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (KATL) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, now nearing completion. After a couple of obstacles during the development process, such as the conversion to Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024 standards, mesh issues, and the airport’s sheer size, they are finally approaching its eventual release date, scheduled to happen sometime around mid-April. While the original plan was to release it before Easter, the developer, Matteo Veneziani, wanted to ensure everything was as polished as possible before it reached the digital storefronts, hence the new tentative release window. ‍ They are on the final “to do” list, only needing to place a few 3D details (GSE, vehicles, trees, and people), export and place custom 3D taxiway signs, add custom approach and taxiway lighting, and complete the animations. It is said to be Matteo’s most detailed work yet, setting a new quality standard. Furthermore, they have also confirmed the existence of an X-Plane 12 version that is on the way, and shared a batch of screenshots from the MSFS version: If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: Atelic Releases Dresden Airport for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Atelic has recently released their rendition of Dresden Airport (EDDC) for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, serving Dresden, Germany, with an average of 838,387 passengers per year. Dresden Airport opened to commercial traffic in 1935, quickly deviating from its civil airport purposes to become a Luftwaffe base, then exclusively military throughout the war, and later a Soviet educational center until its civil reopening in 1957. Between 1955 and 1961, Dresden Airport was home to the VEB Flugzeugwerke Dresden, a publicly-owned aircraft factory that didn’t really succeed, but at least increased the airport’s regional importance and shaped its current design. The unsuccessful factory was repurposed as a maintenance center for Eastern Bloc fighter aircraft and helicopters. After the reunification of Germany, the airport underwent massive expansion, and flights to western European capitals were added to the roster, leading to a sevenfold increase in traffic during the first half of the 1990s and the construction of a second terminal (inaugurated in 1995). In 2001, the current passenger terminal was inaugurated, built on top of what had been an aircraft factory (more of an assembly hall). It’s served by Air Cairo, Corendon Airlines, Eurowings, Lufthansa, Pegasus Airlines, Sky Alps, SunExpress, and Swiss International Air Lines. The scenery features an accurate rendition of the airport, with custom ground textures, an up-to-date ground layout, custom animated ground vehicles, a highly accurate terrain, high-quality PBR textures, custom jetways, a custom GSX profile, and more. It’s available on Contrail for roughly $20.67, requiring at least 4.73 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: iniBuilds Releases New Trailer for L-1011 TriStar
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. iniBuilds has given us another look at their upcoming TriStar Airliner for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, through an official trailer video. This trailer follows last week’s confirmation that the TriStar will launch later in March, as a Marketplace exclusive for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, available on both PC and Xbox. That announcement came with some extended preview content, and you can read more about that here. As with the A340 Airliner, initial availability will be through Marketplace only, and we do not yet know when or if the TriStar will retail through other stores. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Stratoware and Miltech Simulations Release MBB Bo 105 Trailer
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Stratoware and Miltech Simulations have recently released a trailer for their upcoming MBB Bo 105 helicopter for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024. First announced in January as part of a partnership with Miltech Simulations, the MBB Bo 105 is being developed for both Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and Microsoft Flight Simulator 2024, with a planned release window in Q1 2026. The Bo 105 is a twin-engine light utility helicopter, originally developed in West Germany, and is known for its rigid rotor system, enabling some impressive agility and aerobatic capability. According to the developers, the aircraft is being built with input from a real-world Bo 105 pilot, and the audio is being built using real recordings. Multiple variants are planned, including civil, military, and specialised configurations. During FSWeekend 2026, we had the opportunity to take a closer look at the aircraft, speaking with managing director Aditya Sethi. More coverage from that will be published soon. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Navigraph Releases Charts Approach Bundle
entry.summary: Sim: OTHER. Navigraph has released a new “Approach Bundle” for its Charts application, introducing three new tools designed to enhance approach planning. The update, which was first previewed during FSWeekend 2026 last Saturday, is now available in the latest version of Navigraph Charts. At the core of the update are three main features: Approach Feathers, Extended Runway Centerlines, and a new Measurement Tool, each designed to provide a more intuitive overview of approach and navigation data. Approach Feathers offer a graphical representation of approach procedures overlayed on the map, displaying the final approach path, alongside key information such as course, frequency, and identifier. This allows for quicker orientation during arrivals, particularly in higher workload situations, as charts can be referenced seamlessly without needing to open or switch between them individually. The Extended Runway Centerlines feature builds on this by projecting the runway centerline outward on the map, giving you a quick reference. Rounding out the update is the Measurement Tool, which enables the calculation of distance and heading between points directly on the chart. This can be used to check waypoint spacing, estimate shortcuts, or quickly assess alternate routing options, without needing external tools. Together, these features form what Navigraph describes as a more “visual and precise” approach to workflow, where the goal was clarity and ease of use. The update is available now to Navigraph subscribers through the Charts application. Get yourself a subscription here. You can also read more on Navigraph's Blog. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: FlightFactor Teases 777-300ER for X-Plane 12
entry.summary: Sim: X-Plane. FlightFactor has offered a brief teaser on its long-awaited Boeing 777-300ER for X-Plane 12, releasing a set of new preview images on their social channels, alongside a short and witty “Soon™” message. This follows the continued development of the 777 v2 series for XP12, which began with the release of the 777-200ER in beta, and which has now recently expanded with the freighter. That same freighter also received recognition at the 2026 Flight Sim Awards, where it was awarded Best Aircraft for X-Plane. The newly shared images are shot from the exterior, showing off some of the wider artwork and textures. FlightFactor has not shared additional details or a more precise timeline, though the latest suggests that the 777-300ER for X-Plane 12 may not be too far off. View the full post here. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: TriTriSim Announces GFX Airliner Effects Addon for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. TriTriSim has announced GFX Airliner for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, a visual effects enhancement add-on designed to bring more atmosphere to your sim. The announcement includes an official trailer and development update, confirming a planned release date of April 23. TriTriSim says GFX Airliner will provide physics-based visual effects, aiming to simulate atmospheric interaction that is not currently represented in the default simulator. This includes features such as wing condensation, engine vortices, and dynamic spray effects during wet runway operations, reacting in real time to conditions such as humidity, pressure, and aircraft behaviour. The add-on is positioned similarly to Parallel 42’s SimFX and Immersion Packs for MSFS 2020. TriTriSim says GFX is driven by real-time CFD simulations, with the effects appearing only when atmospheric conditions such as humidity, temperature, and angle of attack allow for it, although this can be overridden for cinematic purposes. Planned additions also include contrails and wing fade interaction with clouds. GFX Airliner will be available at an introductory price of $14.99 USD, before increasing to $19.99 USD shortly after release. We will keep you updated on its release. ‍ If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon!

entry.title: Aerosoft/ToLiSS Update A340-600 Pro for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. Aerosoft/ToLiSS have recently released their first update for the newly released A340-600 Pro for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, bringing multiple fixes and improvements based on community feedback. Update 1.0.1, the first so far, improves the waypoint-skipping feature, tweaks in-flight wingflex, and adds drag and fuel-flow improvements at cruise altitude, among other changes. The complete changelog follows below: They also stated that they are already working on further updates, including a wing remodel and certain parts of the fuselage, to enhance the model’s accuracy and quality. Furthermore, they have suggested a community-made cockpit lighting mod if one does not want to wait for the official lighting update, which is currently under development. It’s available on Aerosoft’s store for roughly $80, requiring at least 17 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍

entry.title: SiamFlight Releases Incheon International Airport for MSFS 2020/2024
entry.summary: Sim: FS24. SiamFlight has recently released an early access version of their Incheon International Airport (RKSI) rendition for Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020 and 2024, the largest airport in South Korea and among the busiest in the world, with a yearly average of 70 million passengers! Incheon opened in 2001 to replace Gimpo International Airport as the main airport in South Korea and handle the extra international air traffic that the former airport was unable to handle (as seen during the 1988 Olympics and the early 90s). Construction work began in 1992 on reclaimed land between Yeongjong Island and Youngyu Island, taking 8 years to complete and facing a delay due to an economic crisis. It is the main hub for Korean Air, Asiana Airlines, Jeju Air, and Polar Air Cargo. The current record figures stand at around 71 million passengers a year, with Korean Air accounting for 26% of the market share, followed by Asiana Airlines and Jeju Air with 18.04% and 8.09%, respectively. Destination-wise, Hong Kong is the busiest, with 3,149,768 passengers, followed by Osaka-Kansai (2,840,003) and Tokyo-Narita (2,783,677). The early-access release features a fully modeled terminal 1 and 2 (with the latest expansion), all satellite concourses with accurate architecture, cargo aprons 1 and 2, custom ground textures, custom runway textures, custom jetways, hand-crafted ground markings, custom night lighting, and performance-friendly optimization on both platforms. Future updates will include more detail both externally and internally, a custom GSX Pro profile, custom VDGS for the Xbox/marketplace version, more points of interest, cargo 2 apron warehouse texture improvements, more ground clutter, and more. The developers claim it is a true community-driven project, created in close partnership with the dedicated Korean flight simulation community. Updates will be released for free based on community feedback to keep on improving the product. It’s available on SimMarket for roughly $26.65, requiring at least 3.49 GB of free hard disk space to install. If you enjoy our content and want to support Threshold, consider joining us on Patreon! ‍
